multiscape 1.0.4
  • Reference
  • Changelog

Targets API

Source: R/add_constraint_targets.R
targets.Rd

Define feature-level targets for a planning problem.

Targets are stored in x$data$targets and later translated into linear constraints when the optimization model is built. They are defined at the feature level, but are evaluated against the total contribution generated by selected actions in the final solution.

By default, all actions are allowed to contribute toward target achievement. Optionally, the set of contributing actions can be restricted through the actions argument.

A problem may contain multiple simultaneous targets. Each call to add_constraint_targets_absolute() or add_constraint_targets_relative() appends one or more target definitions to x$data$targets. This makes it possible to combine, for example, relative targets counted only from conservation actions with absolute targets counted only from restoration actions.

Details

Let \(\mathcal{P}\) denote the set of planning units, \(\mathcal{A}\) the set of actions, and \(\mathcal{F}\) the set of features.

Let \(x_{ia} \in \{0,1\}\) denote whether action \(a \in \mathcal{A}\) is selected in planning unit \(i \in \mathcal{P}\).

Let \(c_{iaf}\) denote the contribution of action \(a\) in planning unit \(i\) toward feature \(f\), as interpreted by the model from the stored input data.

A target for feature \(f\) is stored as an absolute threshold \(T_f \ge 0\), together with metadata describing how that threshold was defined. When the optimization model is built, the target is translated into a constraint of the form $$ \sum_{(i,a) \in \mathcal{S}_f} c_{iaf} x_{ia} \ge T_f, $$ where \(\mathcal{S}_f\) is the set of planning unit–action pairs allowed to count toward achievement of the target for feature \(f\).

By default, \(\mathcal{S}_f\) includes all feasible actions. If actions is supplied, then \(\mathcal{S}_f\) is restricted to the actions matching that specification.

Feature and action semantics

Targets are always defined for one or more features.

The features argument determines which features the supplied target values refer to when the targets specification does not already identify features explicitly.

The actions argument does not change the target value itself. It only determines which actions are allowed to contribute toward target achievement when the model is built.

An actions specification may contain:

  • action identifiers from x$data$actions$id,

  • action-set labels from x$data$actions$action_set,

  • or a mixture of both.

A features specification may contain:

  • feature identifiers from x$data$features$id,

  • feature names from x$data$features$name, if present,

  • or a mixture of both, if supported by the resolver.

Stored fields

Each stored target row contains, at minimum:

feature

Feature identifier.

sense

Constraint direction, currently "ge".

target_unit

How the target was defined, for example "absolute" or "relative_baseline".

target_raw

The user-supplied target value before conversion.

basis_total

The baseline total used when converting a relative target into an absolute threshold.

target_value

The final absolute threshold stored for model building.

actions

A text representation of the action subset allowed to contribute.

label

Optional user label.

created_at

Creation timestamp.

Absolute vs relative targets

Absolute targets are stored directly as absolute thresholds: $$ T_f = t_f, $$ where \(t_f\) is the value supplied by the user.

Relative targets are supplied as proportions \(r_f \in [0,1]\) and are converted into absolute thresholds using the current baseline total amount of each feature: $$ T_f = r_f \times B_f, $$ where \(B_f\) is the current total amount of feature \(f\) in the landscape, computed by .pa_feature_totals().

Input format

The targets argument is parsed internally by .pa_parse_targets() and may typically be provided as:

  • a single numeric value recycled to all selected features,

  • a numeric vector aligned to features,

  • a named numeric vector where names identify features,

  • a data.frame with feature identifiers and target values.

If targets does not explicitly identify features, then features = NULL means that all features are targeted.

Developed by Jose Salgado-Rojas, Matias Moreno-Faguett, Nuria Aquilue.

Site built with pkgdown 2.2.0.
